coredata.dc:title: Preparation and surface structure study of novel asymmetric Janus carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yingdong
Wang, Lin
Yang, Zhiwei
Yue, Dongmei
coredata.dc:description: Janus particles, heterogeneous bifacial particles, have gained significant interest in materials science and chemical engineering. Nanoscale fabrication of these particles poses notable challenges. Although mature techniques exist for creating Janus particles from inorganic and polymeric materials, similar advancements in carbon dots (CDs) remain unreported. This paper introduces a novel approach using a liquid–liquid interface method to specifically modify CDs suspended at the biphasic interface, resulting in the formation of carbon dots with a unique Janus-type surface distribution (J-CDs). The surface structure was analyzed using transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, elemental analysis and 13C NMR spectroscopy. By correlating their fluorescence properties with time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) calculations, the relationship between the fluorescent characteristics of J-CDs and their asymmetric Janus surface structures was established, further confirming their unique Janus configuration. Water contact angle tests showed that J-CDs exhibit hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties that differ distinctly from those of traditional amphiphilic CDs when suspended in various solvents and dried on a silicon substrate, underscoring their novel structure. Enhanced surface properties were demonstrated through the stabilization of Pickering emulsions and fabrication of polystyrene microspheres, showcasing the potential of J-CDs to modulate surface characteristics for diverse applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Janus Carbon Dots
Amphiphilicity
Surface Modification
Pickering Emulsion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Janus amphiphilic carbon dots
Preparation and characterization of Janus carbon dots
Model calculations of Janus carbon dots
Surface ativity of Janus carbon dots
Application of Janus carbon dots in the stability and Emulsification of Pickering emulsions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Technology Research and Development Program of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Taishan Industrial Experts Program.
This research was funded by the Taishan Industrial Experts Program and the Key Research and Development Program of Shandong Province , grant number 2019JZZY010356 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the preparation process for R-CDs.
Schematic diagram illustrating the synthesis process of J-CDs.
TEM images, HRTEM photos (insets), and particle size distribution graphs of carbon dots: (a) N-CDs, (b) R-CDs-3, and (c) J-CDs-3.
XPS C 1 s spectra, FTIR spectra and 13C NMR spectra of carbon dots: (a) N-CDs, (b) R-CDs-3, (c) J-CDs-3; (d) Infrared spectra of the carbon dots; (e) 13C NMR spectra of carbon dots.
FTIR spectra of CDs: (a) R-CDs, (b) J-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of dialysis samples at various times: (a) Dialysate (external solution) at 30 h, (b) Dialysate (external solution) at 50 h, (c) Dialysate (external solution) at 70 h, (d) Retentate (internal solution) at 30 h, (e) Retentate (internal solution) at 50 h, (f) Retentate (internal solution) at 70 h.

Fluorescence spectra of carbon dots: (a) R-CDs-1, (b) R-CDs-2, (c) R-CDs-3, (d) R-CDs-4, (e) R-CDs-5; (f) Comparative fluorescence spectra of N-CDs and all R-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm.

Fluorescence spectra of carbon dots: (a) J-CDs-1, (b) J-CDs-2, (c) J-CDs-3, (d) J-CDs-4, (e) J-CDs-5; (f) Comparative fluorescence spectra of N-CDs and all J-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm.

Models of carbon core structures and their corresponding emission peak responses: (a) through (j) depict various configurations of the carbon core models with increasing numbers of modified functional groups; (k) graph showing the relationship between the emission peak values and the number of modified functional groups.

Electron density difference maps (isosurface = 0.001 a.u.). Decreases in electron density are represented in blue, while increases are shown in purple. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Contact angle measurements of CDs before and after modification.
Schematic representation of Janus carbon dot self-assembly and Random carbon dot disordered aggregation during drying.

Photographs and microscopy images of Pickering emulsions: (a) Pickering emulsion stabilized by R-CDs-3, (b) Pickering emulsion stabilized by J-CDs-3, (c)-(d) Polarized light microscopy images of Pickering emulsions stabilized by R-CDs-3 and J-CDs-3, respectively, (e) Bar chart showing interfacial tension measurements for emulsions stabilized by R-CDs-3 and J-CDs-3, (f)-(g) SEM images of R-CDs-3 and J-CDs-3 stabilized emulsions, with insets showing particle size distribution graphs.

head.ce:title: Preparation and surface structure study of novel asymmetric Janus carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Beijing City on Preparation and Processing of Novel Polymer Materials, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Beijing City on Preparation and Processing of Novel Polymer Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Beijing City on Preparation and Processing of Novel Polymer Materials, Beijing 100029, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Organic−Inorganic Composites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Janus particles, heterogeneous bifacial particles, have gained significant interest in materials science and chemical engineering. Nanoscale fabrication of these particles poses notable challenges. Although mature techniques exist for creating Janus particles from inorganic and polymeric materials, similar advancements in carbon dots (CDs) remain unreported. This paper introduces a novel approach using a liquid–liquid interface method to specifically modify CDs suspended at the biphasic interface, resulting in the formation of carbon dots with a unique Janus-type surface distribution (J-CDs). The surface structure was analyzed using transmission electron microscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, elemental analysis and 13C NMR spectroscopy. By correlating their fluorescence properties with time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) calculations, the relationship between the fluorescent characteristics of J-CDs and their asymmetric Janus surface structures was established, further confirming their unique Janus configuration. Water contact angle tests showed that J-CDs exhibit hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties that differ distinctly from those of traditional amphiphilic CDs when suspended in various solvents and dried on a silicon substrate, underscoring their novel structure. Enhanced surface properties were demonstrated through the stabilization of Pickering emulsions and fabrication of polystyrene microspheres, showcasing the potential of J-CDs to modulate surface characteristics for diverse applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Janus particles (JPs), distinguished by their unique amphiphilic properties deriving from their dual-faced structure—one hydrophilic and the other hydrophobic—have drawn significant attention in recent years [1]. Early theoretical work by Binks et al. [2] in 2001 demonstrated that at their amphiphilic peak, the desorption energy of JPs at biphasic interfaces could triple that of homogeneous particles. Subsequent practical applications, discussed by Adeline Perro and colleagues in 2005 [3], leveraged JPs as innovative surfactants. That same year, Yoshiko et al. [4] engineered silica microspheres with Janus configurations, showing potential as novel surfactants due to their bifacial surface properties. Further studies have solidified the high-performance surfactant status of JPs, owing to their superior adsorption energy at interfaces [5,6], which markedly enhances emulsion stability [7,8].

Carbon dots (CDs), known for their sub-10 nm size and quasi-spherical shape, emerged in 2004 and have since sparked broad academic interest [9]. These nanoparticles are celebrated for their robust fluorescence and versatile surface functional groups, such as hydroxyl (–OH), carboxyl (–COOH), and amino (–NH2) groups [10]. Their exceptional photoluminescence makes CDs formidable rivals to traditional semiconductor quantum dots like ZnS/CdSe [11]. Additionally, CDs boast a broad range of applications, from photocatalysis and bioimaging to drug delivery, due to their outstanding physical and chemical properties [12–16].

The development of amphiphilic CDs has been notable. In 2012, Wang et al. [17] employed a plasma-induced method to synthesize amphiphilic CDs from egg yolk, making them suitable for use as fluorescent inks that could adjust fluorescence in diverse media, thanks to their interaction with organic dyes or semiconductor quantum dots. That same year, Fowley and colleagues [18] crafted an amphiphilic polymer by integrating a hydrophilic polyethylene glycol segment with a hydrophobic decyl chain into a polymethyl methacrylate backbone, encapsulating oil-soluble CDs to suggest an alternative to heavy metal-based quantum dots for biomedical use. In 2015, Cho and his team [19] further advanced CD technology by linking hydrophobic hexylamine to hydrophilic CDs via simultaneous N, N'-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) coupling and epoxy ring-opening reactions, forming CDs that could generate highly stable Pickering emulsions. Most recently, in 2019, Ren et al. [20] utilized agricultural crop straw to create CDs with enhanced amphiphilic properties due to their disordered edges and heteroatom defects.

Despite extensive research on amphiphilic CDs, the development of Janus structures within CDs to further enhance their amphiphilic properties remains unexplored. This study employs a liquid–liquid interface method to fabricate amphiphilic carbon dots with a novel Janus-type distribution of hydrophilic and hydrophobic functional groups on their surfaces (J-CDs). Combining experimental and theoretical calculations, we confirm their unique surface structure. Further tests on surface performance confirmed the advantages brought by their unique structure.

All reagents used in the experiments were of analytical grade, and the water was deionized. Citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), octadecylamine (ODA), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), styrene, and potassium persulfate (KPS) were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Xylene, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and ethanol were obtained from Belon Technologies Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Cellulose dialysis bags (500–1000 Da) were provided by Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

6 g of CA and 0.9 g of EDA were mixed in 60 mL of deionized water. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h and then transferred to a hydrothermal autoclave, where it was heated to 200 °C and maintained for 8 h to obtain a brown suspension of CDs. The suspension was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove any undissolved carbonaceous particles. Subsequently, the CDs were purified through 24-hour dialysis using a cellulose dialysis bag (500–1000 Da). The final product was freeze-dried and labeled as N-CDs

0.1 g of N-CDs was dispersed in 200 mL of DMSO, and then 0.05 g of ODA was dissolved in the mixture. The mixture was then transferred to a 500 mL three-necked flask and stirred at 80 °C for 8 h. Afterward, it was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to remove any undissolved particles. The mixture was then dialyzed in ethanol for 24 h, followed by a further 24-hour dialysis in deionized water using a cellulose dialysis bag (500–1000 Da). The final product was obtained by freeze-drying and labeled as R-CDs-1 (0.01 g), R-CDs-2 (0.03 g), R-CDs-3 (0.05 g), R-CDs-4 (0.07 g), and R-CDs-5 (0.10 g), based on the amount of ODA used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Janus amphiphilic carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 g of N-CDs was mixed with 0.1 g of EDC and 0.05 g of NHS in 100 mL of deionized water and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. Simultaneously, 0.05 g of ODA was dissolved in 100 mL of xylene. The solutions were combined and sonicated at 20 % of maximum power for 5 h using an ultrasonic cell disruptor. The resultant emulsion was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min. The top and bottom clear layers were removed, retaining only the middle emulsion layer. This layer was dialyzed in ethanol for 24 h and then in deionized water for another 24 h using a cellulose dialysis bag (500–1000 Da). Finally, J-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying, labeled as J-CDs-1 (0.01 g), J-CDs-2 (0.03 g), J-CDs-3 (0.05 g), J-CDs-4 (0.07 g), and J-CDs-5 (0.10 g), based on the amount of ODA used.

A clean silicon wafer with a polished surface was used as the substrate. The cleaning process involved ultrasonic treatment of the silicon wafer in ethanol and water for 15 min each to remove potential contaminants, followed by drying in an oven at 50 °C for 12 h. For the coating preparation, prepared carbon dots were fully dispersed in the target solvent (water, xylene) at a concentration of 1 mg/ml, and treated with an ultrasonic cell disruptor at 20 % of maximum power for 10 min. Then, 100 µl of the dispersion was pipetted onto the substrate surface and dried in an oven at 50 °C for 24 h to prepare the test coating. Contact angle measurements were conducted by vertically dropping a 25 µl water droplet onto the coating surface and using an instrument to record images and collect data.

A carbon dot suspension (1 mg/ml concentration) was mixed with xylene at a 2:1 vol ratio. The mixture was then sonicated using an ultrasonic cell disruptor at 20 % of maximum power for 10 min to prepare the Pickering emulsion.

In a three-necked flask, styrene (10 g), carbon dots (0.1 g), and deionized water (120 ml) were mixed and stirred at 200 rpm for 30 min. KPS (0.5g) was then added, and the mixture was heated to 75 °C and maintained at this temperature for 8 h, resulting in a suspension of polystyrene (PS) microspheres.

Field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted using a JEOL JEM-2100F at an operating voltage of 200 kV on ultrathin carbon films. Organic elemental analysis was performed using a Vario EL cube from Elementar, Germany. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi, using an Al Kα X-ray source (hv = 1486.6 eV) to explore the elemental composition and corresponding chemical states of CDs. During XPS spectral collection, the survey scans had a pass energy of 100 eV, and high-resolution scans had a pass energy of 20 eV, with the C 1 s peak at 284.8 eV used as a reference. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed using a Thermo Nicolet iS20, with samples prepared via the KBr pellet technique, scanning from 500–4000 cm−1. 13C NMR was performed on an AVANCE III 400 MHz nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) using D2O as the solvent and TMS as the internal standard. Contact angle testing was conducted using a Dataphysics OCA20 instrument from Germany. Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Hitachi F7100 fluorometer from Japan, with an excitation range of 300–500 nm. Both excitation and emission slits were set at 5.0 nm, with a scan speed of 1200 nm/min. The interfacial tension (IFT) between water and oil was measured at 25 °C using a Texas-500 spinning drop tensiometer. Lastly, the morphology and distribution of PS microspheres were examined using a Zeiss Gemini 300 scanning electron microscope (SEM) from Germany.

All calculations were performed using the Gaussian 16 software suite [21]. The ground-state geometrical structures of the CDs were optimized using density functional theory (DFT) with the B3LYP method (Becke, three-parameter, hybrid functional with Lee-Yang-Parr correlation) and def2-SVP basis set [22–25]. The emission energy (wavelength) was calculated as the energy difference between the ground state and the first excited state based on the optimized ground-state geometrical structures, using time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) methods under B3LYP/def2-SVP, incorporating a density-based implicit solvent model (SMD) with water as the solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Janus carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: CDs are notable for their rich surface functional groups that facilitate the addition of diverse functionalities through surface modification. Initially, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method with CA and EDA as precursors. The resultant N-CDs were enriched with carboxylic groups, providing ideal sites for further surface modification. Subsequently, these were functionalized to produce amphiphilic carbon dots via an amide reaction mechanism. Specifically, as depicted in Fig. 1 , N-CDs were combined with ODA in a DMSO solution and heated while stirring. This process triggered a dehydration condensation reaction between the carboxyl and amine groups, leading to the random grafting of octadecyl chains on the surface. After several post-treatment steps, these randomly functionalized carbon dots (R-CDs) displayed a uniform distribution of hydrophilic carboxyl groups and hydrophobic octadecyl chains, exhibiting significant amphiphilic characteristics.

Inspired by Janus particles, creating CDs with a Janus structure could significantly enhance their amphiphilic performance, which is crucial for exploring their structural features and potential applications. The fabrication of Janus carbon dots involves retaining hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxyl groups on one side, while modifying the opposite side with hydrophobic groups through an amide reaction. The masking method [26] facilitates this process by covering half of the particle's surface with a protective agent and then selectively modifying the exposed side. Additionally, the Pickering emulsion method [27] offers an effective approach to prepare Janus microspheres. Typically, solid particles act as stabilizers in a high-temperature emulsion of paraffin and water, with part of each particle's surface submerged in the paraffin phase and the other in the water phase. Upon cooling and solidification, solid microspheres partially coated with paraffin are formed, which, after further modification and post-treatment, yield Janus particles with distinct surface functionalities. Inspired by this technique, we processed a water suspension of CDs and a xylene solution containing ODA in a manner akin to handling a Pickering emulsion, leveraging the nanoparticle properties of CDs to stabilize them at the liquid–liquid interface. At this interface, the CDs exposed to the xylene phase underwent a reaction with ODA, successfully producing J-CDs.

As depicted in Fig. 2 , the process began with the activation of carboxyl groups on N-CDs using EDC/NHS in water to form O-CDs. The aqueous suspension of O-CDs was then blended with the xylene solution of ODA and emulsified to further reinforce the Pickering emulsion formation. The activation by EDC/NHS resulted in O-CDs with NHS functional groups uniformly distributed on their surface. These O-CDs were then suspended at the biphasic interface to form a stable emulsion. Under the influence of amide intermediates, the part of O-CDs exposed to the xylene phase underwent an amide reaction with ODA, achieving a balance of hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties at the interface. Following post-treatment, the J-CDs were obtained, characterized by one side rich in hydrophilic functional groups and the other endowed with hydrophobic groups.

To evaluate the effectiveness of the modification of CDs and the changes in surface functional groups before and after modification, we conducted elemental analysis, TEM, XPS, FTIR, and 13C NMR tests. Table 1 details the differences in elemental composition. Compared to N-CDs, R-CDs-3 and J-CDs-3 exhibited an increase in carbon content and a decrease in oxygen content, indicating that ODA was effectively grafted onto the surface of the CDs through the amidation reaction.

To assess the impact of surface modification on the microstructure of CDs, the morphology of three types of CDs was characterized using TEM. Fig. 3 illustrates that all three CD variants exhibited excellent dispersibility. The average diameters of R-CDs-3 and J-CDs-3 were measured at 3.3 nm and 3.4 nm, respectively, compared to 3.1 nm for N-CDs, indicating negligible differences in size due to the modification methods. HRTEM images revealed distinctive lattice fringe patterns with a spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, characteristic of the (100) crystallographic planes of graphene [28,29].

To elucidate the changes in surface functional groups induced by modification, XPS, FTIR and 13C NMR analyses were conducted on N-CDs, R-CDs-3, and J-CDs-3. These complementary techniques provide a comprehensive understanding of the surface composition and structural changes in CDs. As revealed by the XPS results in Fig. 4 a, four distinct states of carbon are present in N-CDs: COOH (290.01 eV), CO (287.65 eV), C-O/C-N (285.74 eV), and CC/CC (284.58 eV), aligning with previous research [12,30–32]. The detection of CC/CC bonds suggests a graphitic structure, confirming the core composition of CDs. Furthermore, the FTIR spectrum, as shown in Fig. 4d, displays a broad vibrational band between 3250 and 3750 cm−1, indicative of OH stretching vibrations. Additionally, peaks at 1780 and 1703 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of anhydrides and carboxylic/amide CO, respectively, evidencing the presence of carboxyl groups on the CD surfaces, corroborating the XPS results. In the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 4e), a peak at ∼ 160 ppm is assigned to carbonyl groups (CO), such as carboxyl or ester groups, reflecting surface oxidation. Peaks at 30–50 ppm are attributed to aliphatic CH or CC bonds, indicative of non-polar regions within the CD core [32]. The broad and weak signals likely result from surface defects or disorder, consistent with observations from XPS and FTIR. These findings collectively confirm the presence of oxygen-containing groups and a graphitic core structure in the CDs.

In contrast, the XPS, FTIR, and 13C NMR analyses of R-CDs-3 demonstrated notable changes compared to unmodified N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4b, the carbon in R-CDs-3 exists in three distinct forms: CO (288.40 eV), CO/CN (286.09 eV), and CC/CC (284.70 eV). Compared to N-CDs, the CC/CC region is significantly enhanced, indicating an increase in surface carbon content, consistent with the results of elemental analysis. This observation is attributed to the detachment of small molecules adsorbed on the surface during the modification and post-treatment processes, as well as the doping of ODA. Further insights from the FTIR spectra, shown in Fig. 4d, reveal that the peaks 1780 and 1703 cm−1, associated with the stretching vibrations of anhydrides and carboxylic/amide CO groups, remained largely unchanged. This suggests that, apart from the formation of amide bonds, no additional chemical or structural modifications occurred on the CDs’ surface. Additionally, Fig. 4d shows a broad absorption peak within the range of 3300–3700 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH groups. The absorption peaks at 2925 and 2853 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching and bending vibrations of CH from ODA's long alkyl chains, further confirm the successful integration of ODA into the N-CDs structure. Moreover, the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 4e) of R-CDs-3 shows a peak at 40–50 ppm, likely from alkyl chains, indicating further alkylation or non-polar surface modification after the grafting process. This observation is consistent with previous characterizations, supporting the successful doping of ODA.

The distinctions between J-CDs-3 and both N-CDs and R-CDs-3 were further elucidated through XPS, FTIR, and 13C NMR analyses. As shown in Fig. 4c, the carbon in J-CDs-3 exists in three distinct configurations: C=O (287.95 eV), CO/CN (286.36 eV), and CC/CC (284.80 eV). A significant increase in CC/CC content, similar to that observed in R-CDs-3, suggests the successful grafting of ODA onto the surface. In contrast, the substantial decrease in the CO region compared to N-CDs indicates that carboxyl groups participated in reactions facilitated by EDC/NHS. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4d) supports these findings, showing CH bending and stretching vibrations at 2920 and 2853 cm−1, consistent with the presence of ODA. Notably, J-CDs-3 exhibits a tertiary amide CO stretching vibration at 1650 cm−1, which is absent in both N-CDs and R-CDs-3, indicating the occurrence of an indirect amidation reaction mediated by EDC/NHS and the retention of residual NHS groups on the surface. Furthermore, the enhanced COH absorption peak in the 3200–3600 cm−1 range compared to R-CDs reflects an increase in hydroxyl group content. These differences stem from distinct modification processes. For J-CDs, the indirect amidation process catalyzed by EDC/NHS introduces NHS functional groups onto the surface, reducing oxygen content by converting carboxyl groups (COOH) into amide bonds. At the same time, hydroxyl groups (OH) are introduced onto the surface, likely through interactions with water or other polar components during the reaction. These opposing effects—oxygen reduction from NHS functional group incorporation and oxygen increase from hydroxyl group introduction—reach a dynamic balance, resulting in minimal differences in overall oxygen content between R-CDs and J-CDs, as observed in elemental analysis. However, the increased hydroxyl content is clearly reflected in the stronger absorption peak in the 3200–3600 cm−1 range of the FTIR spectrum for J-CDs. The 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 4e) further supports these conclusions. Enhanced signals in the 40–50 ppm region suggest an increase in CH bonds, likely due to alkylation. A stronger peak at ∼ 160 ppm corresponds to carbonyl groups (CO), suggesting surface oxidation or other modifications. These results indicate that during doping and post-treatment processes, surface modifications in J-CDs-3 lead to the loss of small molecules adsorbed on the surface and the introduction of new functional groups.

In summary, J-CDs-3 incorporates octadecyl functional groups and retains NHS groups following modification. The decreased CO signal in the XPS spectrum compared to N-CDs, coupled with increased hydroxyl content and evidence of dynamic surface chemistry, underscores the complexity of the EDC/NHS-mediated modification process. These results confirm successful ODA doping and highlight the unique surface properties of J-CDs-3.

Furthermore, to assess the impact of varying ODA concentrations on the structural modifications of CDs, infrared spectroscopy analyses were performed on R-CDs and J-CDs prepared with different ODA dosages. As depicted in Fig. 5 a and b, an increase in ODA dosage correspondingly enhanced the relative intensities of the C–H bending and stretching vibrations at 2920 cm−1 and 2853 cm−1, indicating a greater amount of ODA grafted onto the CDs' surface. The relative intensities of other absorption peaks remained substantially consistent, suggesting that variations in ODA dosages did not markedly influence other structural aspects of the CDs’ surface.

In summary, elemental analysis, TEM, XPS, FTIR, and 13C NMR assessments confirm the efficacy of both methods in grafting ODA onto the surface of the CDs. The synthesis of R-CDs involved a direct amide reaction without the involvement of EDC/NHS. In contrast, the preparation of J-CDs entailed an indirect amide reaction facilitated by EDC/NHS, leaving some unreacted NHS groups on the surface after the reaction.

Characterizing the structure of Janus nanoparticles, similar in size to CDs, presents significant challenges, as conventional methods like TEM and SEM often fail to reveal the Janus structure clearly [33]. For example, Janus gold nanoparticles are commonly characterized using techniques such as scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) [34], nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) [35], and mass spectrometry (MS) [36]. STM exploits the smooth surface properties of gold nanoparticles to clearly distinguish the modified Janus organic shells due to their distinct surface characteristics. NMR and MS, conversely, are primarily employed to analyze their surface organic ligands. Variations in test data, arising from different modification styles (Janus versus random) and the increasing presence of organic ligands, markedly showcase significant discrepancies. One contributing factor is that the core of the gold nanoparticles does not influence the characterization outcomes of the organic ligands.

Research by Hola et al. [37] demonstrated that a gradual increase in the amount of graphitic nitrogen within the carbon core of CDs leads to a red shift in emission. Similarly, a study by Do et al. [38] revealed that modifying the carbon core of CDs with sulfur and nitrogen atoms results in a red shift in the photoluminescence emission of the carbon dots. Based on these findings, we hypothesize that different surface doping methods will exhibit distinct effects on the fluorescence performance of the carbon core. Consequently, we conducted fluorescence characterization on CDs that had been modified in various ways.

Previous studies [39–41] have shown that CDs synthesized using CA as the carbon source exhibit two primary sources of fluorescence. The first consists of luminescent small molecules dominated by IPCA (5-hydroxy-1,2,3,5-tetrahydropyrrolo [1,2-a] pyridine-7-carboxylic acid), characterized by non-excitation-dependent photoluminescence. The second source, the carbon core, displays excitation-dependent photoluminescence.

To assess the impact of various modification methods on the fluorescence performance of CDs, we initially explored the fluorescence characteristics of N-CDs. Fig. 6 illustrates the fluorescence characterization of N-CDs under different dialysis conditions. Fig. 6a–c show the fluorescence spectra of the external solution at various excitation wavelengths over 30, 50, and 70 h, respectively, while Fig. 6d–f depict the spectra of the internal solution at these times. Observed in Fig. 6a–c, under excitation wavelengths below 370 nm, the external solution primarily shows non-excitation-dependent fluorescence with emission around 440 nm. This is in line with Song et al. [39], indicating the main component is IPCA, and no significant fluorescence is detected in the external solution above 430 nm, suggesting that IPCA-dominant luminescent molecules do not fluoresce in this range. In contrast, Fig. 6d–f demonstrate excitation-dependent fluorescence above 430 nm, primarily emanating from the CDs’ carbon core, corroborating earlier research [39–41]. It is also evident that as dialysis time increases, the fluorescence intensity from this region grows relative to that of the luminescent small molecules, though the peak emission wavelength remains constant, confirming that the luminescent molecules do not influence the carbon core’s fluorescence in this region. This fluorescence characterization affirms that the fluorescence performance of the N-CDs aligns with prior studies [39–41], where the luminescence primarily stems from both IPCA and the carbon core, with the carbon core’s fluorescence predominantly displayed above 430 nm excitation wavelengths.

Further fluorescence characterization was conducted on CDs modified by two distinct methods to investigate the impact of these modifications on the fluorescence performance of the carbon core. Fig. 7 depicts the fluorescence characteristics of R-CDs, with Fig. 7a–e displaying the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs-1 through R-CDs-5 at varying excitation wavelengths. It is apparent from these figures that within the fluorescence range corresponding to the carbon core (excitation wavelengths above 430 nm), there is a noticeable red shift in the emission wavelengths compared to N-CDs (Fig. 6d–f), aligning with the findings of Hola et al. [37]. Additionally, Fig. 7f presents the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs and R-CDs-1 to R-CDs-5 at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm. It can be seen that with minimal ODA doping, there is no significant red shift in the emission wavelength. However, as the ODA concentration increases, a marked red shift becomes evident, especially in R-CDs-3, where the emission peak reaches approximately 500 nm. As the doping level increases in R-CDs-4 and R-CDs-5, the extent of the red shift starts to plateau, indicating that ODA doping influences the overall structure of the carbon dots, which subsequently affects the carbon core, causing the red shift in the emission wavelength to initially increase and then stabilize with higher ODA doping levels in R-CDs.

Fig. 8 depicts the fluorescence characterization of J-CDs, with Fig. 8a to 8e showcasing the fluorescence spectra of J-CDs-1 through J-CDs-5 under various excitation wavelengths. As observed with R-CDs, the figures clearly indicate a red shift in emission wavelengths within the fluorescence range of the carbon core (at excitation wavelengths above 430 nm), when compared to N-CDs (Fig. 6d–f). Furthermore, Fig. 8f presents the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs alongside J-CDs-1 to J-CDs-5 at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm. It can be observed that for J-CDs (Fig. 8f), as the amount of ODA doping increases, the emission peak at an excitation wavelength of 440 nm shifts to 520 nm (J-CDs-3), resulting in a 30 nm red shift compared to N-CDs. However, with further increases in ODA doping, the red shift in J-CDs begins to decrease, indicating that the red shift initially increases and then diminishes. In contrast, for R-CDs (Fig. 7f), the emission peak shifts to 500 nm (R-CDs-3), a 10 nm red shift compared to N-CDs. These two systems exhibit different trends in fluorescence behavior. We hypothesize that these differences arise from the distinct modification methods, resulting in different surface structural distributions on the carbon dots, which subsequently influence the carbon core (random versus Janus). These variations likely influence the electronic states of the carbon core, thereby affecting its fluorescence performance. To validate this hypothesis, we have modeled the carbon core under different modification methods and employed TDDFT to delve into the changes and underlying mechanisms affecting the fluorescence performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model calculations of Janus carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: To validate our hypothesis concerning the correlation between the fluorescence performance of the carbon core and its surface modification methods (Janus versus random), we constructed models of the carbon core under varied surface modification conditions and analyzed their fluorescence properties.

Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, frequently used to model the carbon core of CDs, are based on the assumption that CDs' optical properties relate to the sp2 carbon network within the core [42–44]. These models can replicate or help elucidate the experimental characteristics or trends observed in CDs. For our core model, we selected coronene, a single-layer, small-sized model commonly used in prior research [43,45]. The edge structures of coronene, rich in binding sites, are ideally suited for analyzing the effects of different surface modification methods on the CDs' fluorescence performance. For amine functionalization, we chose methylamine as the amine source. Generally regarded as electron donors, alkylamines during the modification process promote electron transfer from the ligand to the carbon core through the overlap of nitrogen and carbon electron orbitals. This transfer significantly influences the distribution of electron-hole pairs within the core, evident in the observable changes in fluorescence properties [46]. The electron-donating capacity, which increases with the carbon chain length, distinguishes ODA from methylamine. Our primary focus was on examining how various amounts of alkylamine modification on the carbon core surface affect the electron-hole pair distribution patterns and, consequently, the fluorescence performance. Although ODA and methylamine impact these distribution patterns to different extents, they exhibit a consistent trend. Given the constraints of computational cost, using simplified methylamine as a representative model to investigate the basic electron-donating characteristics of ODA proved practical. The computational results obtained from studying different distributions and quantities of methylamine modification on the carbon core surface offer insights that effectively corroborate experimental observations.

Fig. 9 displays the computational results demonstrating the impact of different modification methods on the carbon core's fluorescence performance. Fig. 9a depicts the unmodified carbon core model, Fig. 9b shows the model with its edges fully occupied by amide functional groups, and Fig. 9c–f illustrate models of the carbon core modified randomly along the edges with increasing amide functional groups. Fig. 9g–j represent the models of the carbon core modified in a Janus fashion with increasing amide groups along the edges. Fig. 9k plots the emission peaks calculated using TDDFT against the number of modified functional groups involved. It is clear from Fig. 9k that, compared to unmodified CDs, the amine-functionalized CDs induce a significant red shift in their fluorescence emission peaks, consistent with experimental observations. Further, it is observed that with an increasing number of amine functionalizations, the patterns of emission peak red shifts show significant divergence, especially when comparing random and Janus modifications. Notably, at the same modification level, each Janus-style carbon dot exhibits a more pronounced red shift in its fluorescence emission peak than its random-style counterpart. This finding confirms experimental results and provides strong theoretical support for manufacturing J-CDs as Janus-style entities.

To further elucidate the distinctions between the two types of CDs, electron density difference maps for the S0 → S1 transition were generated with varying numbers of amine functionalizations. Fig. 10 a displays the unmodified carbon core, while Fig. 10b–e depict random modifications with 3, 5, 7, and 9 amine functional groups, respectively. Fig. 10f–i illustrate the Janus modifications with corresponding quantities of amine functional groups, and Fig. 10j shows the carbon core edges fully occupied by amide functional groups. Notably, in the absence of amine functionalization, local excitations of electrons and holes facilitate electron transfer among neighboring holes, with minimal separation observed between electrons and holes. With random modifications, increasing the number of amine functional groups does not induce significant changes in the distribution of electrons and holes. Conversely, with Janus modifications, an increase in the number of amine functional groups markedly enhances the segregation between electrons and holes, as shown in Fig. 10i where most holes are concentrated in the areas doped with amine functional groups, and the majority of electrons are located in the undoped regions.

We posit that with random modifications, despite the electron-donating properties of amine functional groups, their modification does not substantially alter the overall symmetry of the carbon core. Consequently, their impact on the distribution of electrons and holes within the core is minimal. In contrast, the structural changes triggered by Janus modifications significantly disrupt the overall symmetry of the carbon core, markedly influencing the distribution of electrons and holes. This phenomenon is evident in the pronounced red shift of the emission peak in the excited state, induced by Janus modifications, which corroborates previous research linking increased red shifts to charge transfers from amine functional groups to the carbon core [46,47].

In summary, to validate the Janus-type surface structure of J-CDs, we first provided experimental evidence showing that R-CDs and J-CDs exhibit distinct trends in fluorescence performance with increasing amounts of the ODA modifier. While the experimental results are consistent with the theoretical predictions in terms of general trends, the fluorescence differences between R-CDs and J-CDs observed experimentally are less pronounced than those suggested by the computational results. This discrepancy may arise from the steric hindrance of ODA, which prevents or limits its direct interaction with the carbon core surface. Furthermore, through theoretical simulations, we uncovered the underlying mechanisms, revealing that random and Janus-type modifications impact the distribution of electron-hole pairs within the carbon core differently, leading to variations in their fluorescence characteristics. The integration of experimental results and theoretical models supports the conclusion that the J-CDs we synthesized possess Janus-type surface features.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface ativity of Janus carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Contact angle testing is a commonly used method to assess the wettability of solid surfaces [48]. Typically, an angle of 90° serves as the threshold distinguishing hydrophilic from hydrophobic surfaces, with larger angles indicating increased hydrophobicity. This test is frequently employed in nanoparticle research to characterize the wettability of coatings on smooth substrates [49,50]. Specifically for CDs and their composites, contact angle measurements yield intuitive data about their surface properties—hydrophobicity or hydrophilicity [31,51].

In this study, we performed contact angle tests on carbon dot samples that had been variously modified to explore changes in their hydrophobic and hydrophilic characteristics. Water and xylene, used as solvents, prepared suspensions of the modified CDs at identical concentrations that were then applied to a silicon substrate. After drying, the contact angles of the carbon dots on the silicon surface were measured. As demonstrated in Fig. 11 , the contact angle for the untreated silicon substrate was 85.3°, which aligns closely with literature values [52,53]. For N-CDs, owing to their unstable suspension in xylene, only water-based suspensions were used on the silicon substrate, yielding a contact angle of 9.8°, indicative of excellent hydrophilicity. For R-CDs, the contact angle increased with the use of more ODA, and there was negligible disparity between the two suspension types, suggesting that grafting with ODA predominantly enhanced their hydrophobic properties, consistent with prior research [31].

Moreover, the results for J-CDs were distinctly different. The varying treatment methods substantially influenced the contact angles of J-CDs on the silicon substrate. Irrespective of the amount of ODA used, J-CDs exhibited pronounced hydrophilicity after treatment with water suspensions and hydrophobicity after xylene suspensions. This behavior is attributed to the unique Janus structure of J-CDs. We hypothesize that during the drying process of carbon dot suspensions, both J-CDs and N-CDs/R-CDs form aggregates driven by non-covalent interactions, such as hydrogen bonding [54]. The key distinction lies in the aggregation behavior of J-CDs, dictated by their Janus structure. Specifically, J-CDs tend to self-assemble into hierarchical aggregates, with their inner side opposing the solvent's polarity and the outer side aligning with it. This orientation, influenced by the suspension's properties during drying, mirrors prior findings on Janus particles [55,56]. In contrast, R-CDs, lacking a Janus structure, form disordered aggregates without orientation. These differences are reflected in the distinct contact angle patterns observed for J-CDs and R-CDs, as shown in Fig. 12 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Janus carbon dots in the stability and Emulsification of Pickering emulsions
ce:section.ce:para: In Pickering emulsions, solid particles act as stabilizers, typically offering higher stability than those stabilized with conventional surfactants [57]. Previous research has demonstrated that amphiphilic CDs effectively stabilize Pickering emulsions [19,20]. Due to their distinctive structure, Janus nanoparticles outperform homogeneous nanoparticles in stabilizing emulsions and maintaining long-term stability [6].

This study explored the amphiphilic property differences caused by structural variations in two types of modified CDs through stability tests on Pickering emulsions. N-CDs, lacking hydrophobic properties, were unable to form stable emulsions. We utilized R-CDs-3 and J-CDs-3 as stabilizers to prepare toluene/water Pickering emulsions, monitoring their stability over time (Fig. 13 a and b). Initial findings indicated that both emulsions were initially stable. However, over time (24 h to 144 h), emulsions using J-CDs-3 showed delayed separation and were denser compared to those using R-CDs-3. Significantly, after six months, the J-CDs-3 stabilized emulsion remained stable, whereas the R-CDs-3 emulsion had completely separated, underscoring the superior stability of J-CDs. Polarized light microscopy of both emulsions after 144 h revealed that the emulsion with R-CDs contained larger droplets, whereas those with J-CDs-3 were significantly smaller. These observations, coupled with surface tension tests (results shown in Fig. 13c–e), further validated the exceptional amphiphilic performance of J-CDs, attributable to their unique Janus structure, aligning with prior findings on Janus nanoparticles [6].

Our research confirmed that both modified carbon dots, R-CDs and J-CDs, effectively stabilize Pickering emulsions and therefore serve as suitable stabilizers for the emulsion polymerization of PS. Notably, J-CDs, with their unique Janus structure, exhibited enhanced emulsion stability compared to R-CDs. To further investigate performance differences in emulsion polymerization, we used both types of CDs as stabilizers to prepare PS microspheres. Attempts to use N-CDs as surfactants failed to yield spherical structures, highlighting their inadequacy as surfactants. In contrast, using R-CDs-3 and J-CDs-3 as emulsifiers, we successfully produced PS microspheres. SEM analysis (Fig. 13f and g) indicated that PS microspheres prepared with R-CDs-3 had a wide size distribution, ranging from tens to hundreds of nanometers, whereas J-CDs-3 resulted in predominantly nanoscale microspheres. We believe that both R-CDs-3 and J-CDs-3 can initially form uniform droplets in emulsion polymerization. However, as shown in Fig. 13a and b, R-CDs-3 exhibits poorer emulsion stability, leading to droplet coalescence during polymerization. This coalescence leads to a wider size distribution in the final PS microspheres. In contrast, J-CDs-3, with superior surface stability, maintains more uniform droplet sizes throughout polymerization.

This study began with N-CDs and successfully synthesized J-CDs featuring distinctly distributed surface functional groups through a liquid–liquid interface method. It systematically examined their fluorescence properties, surface characteristics, and potential applications. The fluorescence characteristics of J-CDs were compared with R-CDs under varying ODA concentrations, incorporating TDDFT calculations to confirm the asymmetric surface structure of J-CDs. The TDDFT calculations explicitly revealed the differences in fluorescence performance between the two types of carbon dots, attributing these variations to the distinct effects of various surface modification methods on the electron-hole distribution within the carbon core. Additionally, contact angle tests revealed significant differences in surface wettability between J-CDs and R-CDs, consistent with existing literature on the contact angle outcomes for Janus particles. J-CDs also demonstrated enhanced stability as emulsifiers in the preparation of Pickering emulsions, and the PS microspheres they facilitated exhibited a narrower and more uniform particle size distribution, suggesting advantages in emulsion polymerization. In conclusion, J-CDs, with their unique Janus structure, surpass R-CDs in surface performance and applications, showcasing extensive potential in the field of multifunctional materials.

Yingdong Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lin Wang: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhiwei Yang: Visualization, Supervision, Formal analysis. Dongmei Yue: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Dongmei Yue reports financial support was provided by Shandong Provincial Department of Science and Technology. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Taishan Industrial Experts Program.
